coredata.dc:title: Acoustic cavitation nuclei survive the apparent ultrasonic destruction of Albunex® microspheres
coredata.dc:creator: Brayman, Andrew A.
Miller, Morton W.
coredata.dc:description: The hypothesis tested was that gas bodies capable of nucleating violent cavitation activity in vitro would survive the rapid disruption of Albunex® microspheres by 1-MHz ultrasound. Human erythrocyte hemolysis was used as a proxy measure of cavitation. Fluid (5% human serum albumin [HSA]) with or without Albunex® (ALX) was exposed or sham-exposed to 1-MHz ultrasound (P+ = 1.25 ± 0.01 MPa, P− = 0.81 ± 0.01 MPa; I SPTP ∼ 35 W/cm2) for 60 s using 10-μs pulses and a duty factor of 0.5. An equal volume of whole human blood was then added to the fluid, followed by a second 60-s treatment. Insonation of cell suspensions prepared in previously sham-exposed HSA + ALX fluid produced about 4% hemolysis, a level significantly greater than in the controls. Insonation of cell suspensions prepared in previously insonated HSA + ALX fluid produced about 0.4% hemolysis; this also differed significantly from the controls. The data thus support the hypothesis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Cavitation nuclei
Albunex® microspheres
Ultrasonic hemolysis

xocs:doc.xocs:rawtext: ELSEVIER Ultrasound in Med. & Biol., Vol. 23, No. 5, pp. 793-796, 1997 Copyright 1997 World Federation for Ultrasound in Medicine & Biology Printed in the USA. All rights reserved 0301-5629/97 $17.00 + .00 PH S0301-5629(97)00008-2 *Original Contribution ACOUSTIC CAVITATION NUCLEI SURVIVE THE APPARENT ULTRASONIC DESTRUCTION OF ALBUNEX@ MICROSPHERES ANDREW A. BRAYMAN and MORTON W. MILLER Department of Obstetrics and Gynecology, School of Medicine and Dentistry, The University of Rochester, Rochester, NY, USA (Received 10 September 1996; in final form 3 Februarv 1997) Abstract-The hypothesis tested was that gas bodies capable of nucleating violent cavitation activity in vitro would survive the rapid disruption of Albunexmicrospheres by 1-MHz ultrasound. Human erythrocyte hemolysis was used as a proxy measure of cavitation. Fluid (5% human serum albumin [HSA ]) with or without Albunex' (ALX) was exposed or sham-exposed to 1-MHz ultrasound (P+ = 1.25 0.01 MPa, P- = 0.81 0.01 MPa; Isprp - 35 W/cmz) for 60 s using 10-its pulses and a duty factor of 0.5. An equal volume of whole human blood was then added to the fluid, followed by a second 60-s treatment. Insonation of cell suspensions prepared in previously sham-exposed HSA + ALX fluid produced about 4% hemolysis, a level significantly greater than in the controls. Insonation of cell suspensions prepared. in previously insonated HSA + ALX fluid produced about 0.4% hemolysis; this also differed significantly from the controls. The data thus support the hypothesis. 1997 World Federation for Ultrasound in Medicine and Biology. Key Words: Cavitation nuclei, Albunex' microspheres, Ultrasonic hemolysis. INTRODUCTION Acoustic cavitation has long been known to lyse cells in vitro (see review by Miller et al. 1996). The bulk of the evidence (see Carstensen et al. 1993) supports the view that cell lysis arises primarily via inertial cavitation (i.e., involving the growth and violent collapse of microbubbles) rather than noninertial cavitation processes (e.g., bubble translation, radiation forces exerted on nearby particles, etc.). Albumin-stabilized microspheres are acoustically labile. The backscatter produced by the microspheres is diminished by exposure to even modest acoustic pressures (Vandenberg 1990; Vandenberg and Melton 1994; Wray et al. 1991), suggesting that gas leaves the microspheres after the albumin shells have been broken or otherwise disrupted by ultrasound. Albunex(ALX) microspheres can nucleate acoustic cavitation activity in vitro (Azadniv et al. 1995; Brayman et al. 1995, 1996; Miller et al. 1995) and in vivo (Dalecki et al. 1997). A question of considerable interest is whether cavitation-induced cell lysis arises as a consequence of the initial disruption of the microspheres or Address correspondence to: Dr. A. A. Brayman, University of Rochester Medical Center, Box 668, 601 Elmwood Avenue, Rochester, NY 14642, USA. as a consequence of "derivative microbubbles" created when the microspheres' albumin shells are compromised and their gas escapes. There is some evidence also (I. R. S. Makin, personal communication) that ALX suspensions remain highly attenuating even after insonation has cleared the fluid of visible turbidity. If so, there must be a large number of bubbles left in the fluid. Whether these are "residual" ALX microspheres surviving the initial insonation (e.g., by being out of the beam) or are "derivative" microbubbles is not clear. The goal of the experiments described here was to determine if previously insonated ALX microsphere suspensions can produce a significant increase in cavi tation activity relative to control media, using hemolysis as a proxy measure of cavitation activity. The specific hypothesis tested was that cavitation nuclei would persist in aqueous media following insonation of ALX microspheres sufficient to cause apparent destruction of the original microspheres. MATERIALS AND METHODS Human blood was obtained from a single volunteer, whose hematocrit was consistently about 48%. Blood was drawn and anticoagulated with 14 mL of 793 794 Ultrasound in Medicine and Biology Volume 23, Number 5, 1997 CPDA-1 per 100 ml, of blood immediately before each experiment, bubbled with 100% of OZ and then maintained on ice. These treatments minimize time-dependent changes in cell sensitivity to ultrasonic hemolysis (Azadniv et al. 1995). Round-bottom polystyrene tubes (Corning No. 25760; 16 x 125 mm) were used to contain the host fluid and cell suspensions during treatment. Tubes were rotated at 100 rpm during treatment. When used, the tubes contained either 1 mL of the fluid to be pretreated (fluid height ^-11 mm) or 2 ml, of cell suspension (1 mL each of pretreated fluid and whole blood; fluid height -19 mm). In each case, the tube was positioned such that the fluid column was roughly centered in the ultrasound beam. Although the height of the 2-mL fluid column was greater than the -6dB beam diameter (see below), earlier experiments (Brayman et al. 1992) using the same setup and a 3-mL fluid volume showed that nearly 100% of the insonated cells could be lysed. Thus, considerable mixing of the tube contents occurs during insonation with tube rotation. Ultrasound was produced by a 2.5-cm-diameter unfocused ceramic transducer operating at 1.02 MHz. Dosimetry was accomplished using an Imotec needle hydrophone to measure acoustic pressures. Samples were positioned 7.5 cm from the transducer face, where the -6-dB intensity beam diameter was 7.7 mm. All insonated samples (pretreated fluids and/or cell suspensions) were exposed to ultrasound for 60 s, using 10-jus pulses and a duty factor of 0.5. The mean standard error peak positive and peak negative acoustic pressures applied in the four experiments were: P+ = 1.25 0.01 MPa, P- = 0.81 0.01 MPa. The SPTP intensity was -35 W/cm2. A commercial 5% human serum albumin (HSA) solution served as the pretreated host fluid into which whole blood was ultimately added. For some samples, 3.6 vol% of ALX was added to the HSA immediately prior to the pretreatment. Upon dilution with the whole blood, the nominal ALX concentration was 1.8 vol%. Each of the four replicate experiments conducted involved six experimental treatments. These were: (1) a "master control," in which HSA was sham-inso nated, following which whole blood was added and also sham-insonated; (2) a "reagent control," in which HSA + ALX were sham-insonated, following which whole blood was added and also sham-insonated; (3) a "sonochemical control," in which HSA + ALX were insonated, following which whole blood was added and sham-insonated; (4) a "positive control," in which HSA + ALX were sham-insonated, following which whole blood was added and insonated; (5) an "induced microbubble control," in which HSA was insonated, following which whole blood was added and also insonated; (6) an experimental treatment, in which HSA + ALX were insonated, following which whole blood was added and also insonated. Hemolysis was determined by spectrophotometric assay of hemoglobin in 500-pL aliquots of supernatant fractions obtained after sample centrifugation. The re agent blank was a 1:1 mixture of plasma and HSA. Standards representing 20% hemolysis were prepared using 100-uL aliquots of the 1:1 mixture of whole blood and HSA, supplemented with 400 pL of 1:1 plasma + HSA. Where small-magnitude negative values of hemolysis were obtained, the optical density of the sample supernatants were somewhat less than that of the blank. RESULTS When the fluid to be pretreated contained ALX, it appeared turbid. When sonicated, the visible turbidity cleared almost instantaneously. The hemolysis data ob tained after whole blood was mixed with the pretreated fluids are summarized in Table 1. These show that there was a consistent elevation in hemolysis in the positive control treatment (HSA + ALX were shaminsonated, whole blood added and insonated). Approximately 4% of the cells were lysed. Similarly, there was a consistent elevation in hemolysis in the experimental treatment group (HSA + ALX insonated, whole blood added and also insonated). In three of the four experiments, these were the only treatment groups in which hemolysis was greater than in the master control (HSA sham-exposed, whole blood added and also sham-exposed; see Table 2 for statistical test results). Statistical evaluation of the experimental means for each treatment group, relative to the master control, show that only the positive control and experimental treatment produced significant hemolysis. Preinsonation of ALX-containing medium was without lytic effect on cells subsequently sham-exposed in that medium,,and insonation of the cells without ALX produced no lysis. DISCUSSION The data supported the hypothesis. When cells were insonated in the presence of ALX, about 4% of the cells were lysed (Table 1). When cells were mixed with preinsonated ALX suspensions and the mixture then insonated, hemolysis was lower (-0.4%; Table 1), but was significantly greater than that in the master control treatment (Table 2). The rapid visual disappearance of turbidity due to ALX upon insonation suggests that most of the microspheres are destroyed quickly. Whether it is the initial Survival of cavitation nuclei A. A. BRAYMAN and M. W. MILLER 795 Table 1. Levels of hemolysis (percent) produced by the various experimental treatments. Within experiment 1, there were n = 5 observations (i.e., independent samples) per treatment. Within experiments 2-4, there were n = 6 independent samples per treatment. Approx. SPTP intensity Hemolysis (%) (W/cm2) Host fluid Cell-free Treatment Expt.l Expt. 2 Expt.3 Expt.4 Mean constituents pretreatment of cell Mean -_ SEM Mean SEM Mean SEM Mean SEM of SD SEM of fluid suspension means HSA 0 0 0.01 i- 0.15 0.00 0.01 0.00 0.02 0.00 0.01 0.003 0.005 0.003 HSA + ALX 0 0 -0.13 0.04 0.00 0.01 0.04 0.03 0.04 0.03 -0.013 0.081 0.040 HSA + ALX 35 0 -0.07 0.05 -0.01 0.01 0.04-0.04 0.11 0.04 0.018 0.076 0.038 HSA + ALX 0 35 3.80 0.12 4.27 0.22 4.01 0.53 4.31 0.33 4.098 0.239 0.119 HSA 35 35 -0.07 0.05 0.01 0.01 -0.01 0.02 0.06 0.03 -0.003 0.054 0.027 HSA + ALX 35 35 0.47 0.08 0.46 0.13 0.16 --0.06 0.48 -0.06 0.393 0.155 0.078 destruction of the ALX microspheres or the subsequent nucleation of the medium with unstabilized, "derivative" nuclei which produces most of the hemolytic activity cannot be answered unequivocally from the present data. However, some inferences may be drawn. These experiments used blood of approximately 50% hematocrit. When diluted 1:1 with HSA, the cell concentration was therefore ^-3.1 X 109 cells/mL, cor responding to a total cell number of -6.2 X 109 cells/ tube. The ALX microsphere concentration as supplied is ---4.2 X 108/mL (Bleeker et al. 1990), and the total number of ALX bubbles per tube was ^-1.5 X 10' (i.e., 0.018 mL ALX/mL fluid X 2 mL fluid/tube X 4.2 X 108 microspheres/mL of ALX). If the microspheres initially present produced most of the observed hemolysis (-4% in positive control; -2.5 X 108 cells lysed), then ^16.5 cells were lysed per microsphere. The volume of cell suspension containing 16.5 cells was -5.33 X 10-'S m3; hence, if ALX microspheres lyse cells upon their ultrasonic destruction, then the "kill zone" extends about 11 ym from the micro sphere. However, if the ALX microspheres were directly responsible for cell lysis, one might expect that a dose-response relationship would exist between initial ALX concentration and hemolysis. There seems to be none (Miller et al. 1995). This observation suggests that lytic activity is dominated by unstabilized cavitation nuclei created upon the ultrasonic disruption of the original ALX microspheres. The difficulty in accepting this argument is that one might expect that the number of unstabilized nuclei created would be at least coarsely proportional to the initial number of ALX microspheres. The results of the present study indicate that the preinsonated HSA + ALX medium retains about 10% of the lytic activity of non-preinsonated HSA + ALX. If unaltered ALX bubbles persist from the initial insonation to the second, and if activity were proportional to ALX bubble concentration, then it is almost certain that visible turbidity would remain in the medium following the first insonation. We were unable to detect any such turbidity "by eye." This result, taken to Table 2. Tests of the statistical hypothesis that experimental treatment produces a significant elevation in the levels of hemolysis relative to the master control group (i.e., sham-insonated HSA with sham-insonated cells). All tests were one-sided t-tests. Comparisons within experiment 1 involved 8 degrees of freedom, comparisons within experiments 2-4 involved 10 degrees of freedom, and comparison of experimental means involved 6 degrees of freedom. Approx. SPTP intensity Treatment mean greater than master control? (W/cm2) (p-value; t-test) Cell-free Treatment Mean Host fluid pretreatment of cell of constituents of fluid suspension Expt. 1 Expt. 2 Expt. 3 Expt. 4 means HSA 0 0 HSA + ALX 0 0 NS NS NS NS NS HSA + ALX 35 0 NS NS NS 0.013 NS HSA + ALX 0 35 -0.005 -0.005 -0.005 -0.005 40.001 HSA 35 35 NS NS NS 0.045 NS HSA + ALX 35 35 0.015 -0.005 -0.017 <-0.005 -0.05 796 Ultrasound in Medicine and Biology Volume 23, Number 5, 1997 gether with the above considerations, suggest that: (1) insonation of the ALX microspheres disrupts them almost immediately. The lack of a dose-response relationship between the initial ALX concentration and cell lysis suggests that this initial disruption is not a strong contributor to cell lysis; (2) the "derivative" microbubbles created by the disruption of the ALX microspheres are the primary contributors to cell lysis. However, the number of these "derivative" microbubbles formed upon the disruption of the ALX microspheres must be quite small-perhaps as few as oneor else a dose-response relationship would again be expected. Acknowledgements-This work was supported by NIH grant No. R37-CA39230-22. REFERENCES Azadniv M, Doida Y, Miller MW, Brayman AA, Meltzer, RS. Temporality in ultrasound-induced cell lysis in vitro. Echocardiography 1995;13:45-55. Bleeker HJ, Shung KK, Barnhart JL. Ultrasonic characterization of Albunexe, a new contrast agent. J Acoust Soc Am 1990; 87:1792-1797. Brayman AA, Azadniv M, Cox C, Miller MW. Hemolysis of Albu nexsupplemented, 40% hematocrit human erythrocytes in vitro by 1 MHz pulsed ultrasound: Acoustic pressure and pulse length dependence. Ultrasound Med Biol 1996;22:927-938. Brayman AA, Azadniv M, Makin IRS, Miller MW, Carstensen EL, Child SZ, Raeman CH, Meltzer RS, Everbach EC. Effect of a stabilized microbubble contrast agent on hemolysis of human erythrocytes exposed to high-intensity pulsed ultrasound. Echocardiography 1995;12:13-22. Brayman AA, Doida Y, Miller MW. Apparent contribution of respiratory gas exchange to the in vitro "cell density effect" in ultrasonic cell lysis. Ultrasound Med Biol 1992;18:701-714. Carstensen EL, Kelly P, Church CC, Brayman AA, Child SZ, Raeman CH, Schery L. Lysis of erythrocytes by exposure to CW ultrasound. Ultrasound Med Biol 1993;19:147-165. Dalecki D, Raeman CH, Child SZ, Cox C, Francis CW, Meltzer RS, Carstensen EL. Hemolysis in vivo from exposure to pulsed ultrasound. Ultrasound Med Biol 1997;23:307-313. Miller MW, Azadniv M, Doida Y, Brayman AA. Effect of a stabilized microbubble contrast agent on CW ultrasound-induced red blood cell lysis in vitro. Echocardiography 1995;12:1-12. Miller MW, Miller DL, Brayman AA. A review of in vitro bioeffects of inertial cavitation from a mechanistic perspective. Ultrasound Med Biol 1996;22:1131-1154. Vandenberg BF. Reproducibility of albumin microbubble backscatter measurement: Influence of acoustic output [abstract]. Clin Res 1990;38:887A. Vandenberg BF, Melton HG. Acoustic lability of albumin microspheres. J Am Soc Echocardiogr 1994;7:582-589. Wray RA, Quinones MA, Zoghbi WA, Cheirif J. Relation of mean pixel intensity to concentration of sonicated albumin microspheres: Effects of ultrasound system settings. Clin Cardiol 1991;14:23-28.

head.ce:title: Acoustic cavitation nuclei survive the apparent ultrasonic destruction of Albunex® microspheres

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Obstetrics and Gynecology, School of Medicine and Dentistry, The University of Rochester, Rochester, NY, USA

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Address correspondence to: Dr. A. A. Brayman, University of Rochester Medical Center, Box 668, 601 Elmwood Avenue, Rochester, NY 14642, USA.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The hypothesis tested was that gas bodies capable of nucleating violent cavitation activity in vitro would survive the rapid disruption of Albunex® microspheres by 1-MHz ultrasound. Human erythrocyte hemolysis was used as a proxy measure of cavitation. Fluid (5% human serum albumin [HSA]) with or without Albunex® (ALX) was exposed or sham-exposed to 1-MHz ultrasound (P+ = 1.25 ± 0.01 MPa, P− = 0.81 ± 0.01 MPa; I SPTP ∼ 35 W/cm2) for 60 s using 10-μs pulses and a duty factor of 0.5. An equal volume of whole human blood was then added to the fluid, followed by a second 60-s treatment. Insonation of cell suspensions prepared in previously sham-exposed HSA + ALX fluid produced about 4% hemolysis, a level significantly greater than in the controls. Insonation of cell suspensions prepared in previously insonated HSA + ALX fluid produced about 0.4% hemolysis; this also differed significantly from the controls. The data thus support the hypothesis.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Temporality in ultrasound-induced cell lysis in vitro
Ultrasonic characterization of Albunex®, a new contrast agent
Hemolysis of Albunex®-supplemented, 40% hematocrit human erythrocytes in vitro by 1 MHz pulsed ultrasound: Acoustic pressure and pulse length dependence
Effect of a stabilized microbubble contrast agent on hemolysis of human erythrocytes exposed to high-intensity pulsed ultrasound
Apparent contribution of respiratory gas exchange to the in vitro “cell density effect” in ultrasonic cell lysis
Lysis of erythrocytes by exposure to CW ultrasound
Hemolysis in vivo from exposure to pulsed ultrasound
Effect of a stabilized microbubble contrast agent on CW ultrasound-induced red blood cell lysis in vitro
A review of in vitro bioeffects of inertial cavitation from a mechanistic perspective
Reproducibility of albumin microbubble backscatter measurement: Influence of acoustic output [abstract]
Relation of mean pixel intensity to concentration of sonicated albumin microspheres: Effects of ultrasound system settings
